content:
Software Development
46% of all new code is AI-generated. The developers writing it don’t trust it. The companies shipping it don’t care.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

“The code works. I just don’t know why” — Photo by Danial Igdery on UnsplashAnd somehow, nobody finds that weird?
Let me tell you about the weirdest moment of my week.
I was building this small side project — nothing fancy, just a task tracker to learn React. I opened Cursor, typed a prompt, and watched it spit out an entire authentication system. Login page, JWT tokens, password hashing, the whole thing. It took maybe 45 seconds.
I stared at it. It looked right. It ran without errors. I deployed it.
And then this thought hit me like a cold shower: I have absolutely no idea what half of this code does.
I didn’t write it. I didn’t debug it. I just… accepted it. Because the vibes were good.
Turns out, I’m not alone in this. Not even close.

We’re All Doing This Now
Here’s a stat that messed with my head: 92% of developers in the US now use AI coding tools every single day. Ninety-two percent. That’s not early adopters anymore. That’s basically everyone.
But here’s the part that should make you uncomfortable — only 29% of those developers actually trust the code these tools produce. Read that again. Almost everyone is using it. Almost nobody trusts it. And we’re all just… fine with that?
It gets worse. GitHub says 46% of all new code being written right now is AI-generated. Nearly half. We’re building the apps people use for banking, healthcare, dating, literally everything — and half of it was written by a machine that sometimes confidently puts your API keys in plain text.
This isn’t theoretical paranoia, by the way. Earlier this year, a vibe-coded app got breached. 1.5 million API keys exposed. 35,000 email addresses leaked. The developer admitted they hadn’t manually written a single line. They prompted, deployed, and prayed. The prayer didn’t work.

The Part That Actually Scares Me
Okay so here’s where it gets really uncomfortable.
Stanford ran a study. Actual controlled experiment, not some LinkedIn poll. They found that developers who used AI tools wrote less secure code than developers who didn’t. That alone is bad. But the kicker? The AI-assisted developers were more confident that their code was secure.
Let that sink in. The tool made them worse AND made them feel better about being worse.
There’s another study that hit even harder. METR ran a trial with experienced open-source developers — real engineers, not students like me — working on real codebases. The developers using AI were measurably 19% slower on complex tasks. But when they were asked afterwards, they believed AI had made them faster. Even after seeing the data, some still didn’t believe it.
95% of developers report feeling productive while producing lower-quality code. The vibes are immaculate. The results are not.

And It’s Already Breaking Things
Daniel Stenberg — the guy who created cURL, which is basically in every device you’ve ever touched — shut down his project’s bug bounty program this January. Not because of money. Because AI-generated vulnerability reports were flooding it with garbage. Actual security researchers couldn’t get heard over the noise.
The Tailwind CSS team watched their downloads climb while documentation traffic dropped 40% and revenue fell 80%. Developers are using their framework more than ever. They’re just not reading the docs. AI reads them instead. Sometimes wrong.
Steve Ruiz, who built tldraw, started auto-closing ALL external pull requests. Not just AI-generated ones. All of them. Because maintainers literally can’t tell which contributions are from humans and which are AI slop anymore.
The open-source ecosystem that the entire software industry depends on is getting buried in noise. And the tools generating that noise? They’re worth billions. Cursor went from $1 billion to $2 billion in revenue in three months flat.

So What Does This Mean If You’re Just Starting Out?
This is where it gets personal for me.
I entered the tech world at probably the strangest possible time. Every senior developer I talk to learned by struggling through documentation and Stack Overflow threads at 2 AM. They built muscle memory for debugging. They understand why code works, not just that it works.
Me? I’m being told to describe what I want in plain English and let Claude or Copilot handle the rest. Shopify’s CEO literally told his employees they have to prove a task can’t be done by AI before asking for more people. That’s the energy companies are bringing into 2026.
And look — I’m not saying AI tools are bad. They’re genuinely incredible for boilerplate, for scaffolding, for the boring repetitive stuff that used to eat your whole afternoon. I use them every day.
But there’s a difference between using a calculator after you understand math and using a calculator instead of understanding math. I feel like my generation is being handed the calculator on day one and told to run.
The uncomfortable question I keep coming back to is this:
Am I learning to code? Or am I learning to not code — really well?

The Bottom Line
We’ve built an industry where the tools are faster than ever, adoption is nearly universal, and the people using these tools don’t trust what comes out. Developers feel more productive while producing worse results. The open-source ecosystem is drowning in AI-generated noise. And freshers like me are entering a job market that’s being reshaped before we even understand its original shape.
I don’t have a clean answer here. I wish I did.
But I think the first step is at least being honest about the gap — between how productive we feel and how productive we actually are. Between the speed of shipping and the quality of what we ship. Between the vibes and the reality.
Because right now, the vibes are carrying a lot of weight. And I’m not sure the foundation underneath can hold it.
